name: Health & Safety Risk Assessment
purpose: Delivers a comprehensive workplace risk assessment covering all hazards present in a cafe environment — from scalding steam wands and slippery floors to manual handling of deliveries and repetitive strain from tamping. This block ensures legal compliance with the Health and Safety at Work etc. Act 1974 and the Management of Health and Safety at Work Regulations 1999, while building a proactive safety culture that protects staff, customers, and the business.

detailed_objectives: Conduct a full hazard identification survey covering every area of the cafe: front of house, bar, kitchen, storage, toilets, and exterior
Assess each hazard for likelihood and severity to determine risk rating
Document control measures for every identified risk, assigning responsibility and review dates
Create a living risk assessment document that is reviewed and updated at least annually or when circumstances change
Ensure all staff are briefed on relevant risks and control measures during induction and at regular intervals
secondary_objectives: Reduce workplace accidents and near-misses through proactive hazard management
Minimise employer liability and insurance costs through documented due diligence
Build staff confidence by demonstrating that their safety is taken seriously
Create a foundation for fire safety (B184), licensing (B185), and compliance culture (B186) blocks

why_this_matters: Under the Health and Safety at Work etc. Act 1974, every employer has a legal duty to ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, the health, safety, and welfare of all employees. The Management of Health and Safety at Work Regulations 1999 require every employer with five or more employees to conduct and record a suitable and sufficient risk assessment. Even cafes with fewer than five employees must conduct a risk assessment — they simply are not required to record it in writing, although best practice strongly recommends doing so. The Health and Safety Executive (HSE) can inspect any workplace at any time, and a cafe without a documented risk assessment faces enforcement action ranging from an Improvement Notice to prosecution. In the hospitality sector, the most common hazards include burns and scalds (from steam wands, hot water, and cooking equipment), slips, trips and falls (wet floors, cables, uneven surfaces), manual handling injuries (lifting deliveries, moving furniture), cuts (from knives, broken crockery), and electrical hazards (faulty equipment, overloaded sockets). A single workplace accident can result in HSE investigation, potential prosecution, personal injury claims, increased insurance premiums, loss of key staff to injury, and reputational damage. For a small cafe, a successful personal injury claim or HSE prosecution can be financially devastating.

application_logic.L1: IMMEDIATE (0–2 weeks). Week 1: Download the HSE risk assessment template (available free from hse.gov.uk) or use the template provided in T183-01. Walk through every area of the cafe with a notebook: bar area, kitchen/prep, storage, customer seating, toilets, entrance/exit, and any outdoor areas. For each area, list every hazard you can identify — hot surfaces, sharp objects, wet floors, heavy items at height, electrical equipment, trip hazards, chemical cleaning products. For each hazard, note who might be harmed, how, and what controls are already in place. Rate each risk as high, medium, or low. Week 2: For each medium and high risk, document specific control measures: what needs to be done, who is responsible, and by when. Post a summary of key risks and controls in the staff area. Brief all current staff on the risk assessment findings. File the completed risk assessment and note the review date.
application_logic.L2: SYSTEM BUILD (2–8 weeks). Develop a Health and Safety SOP library covering the top risks: burns and scalds prevention, manual handling technique, slip prevention and wet floor procedures, chemical handling (COSHH), and electrical safety. Introduce a near-miss reporting system — a simple form where staff can record incidents that nearly caused harm. Near-miss data is invaluable for identifying emerging risks before they cause harm. Conduct specific risk assessments for vulnerable groups: pregnant workers, young workers (under 18), and anyone with a disability. Review insurance policies to ensure coverage aligns with identified risks. Schedule quarterly risk assessment reviews. Train a designated member of staff as the health and safety lead.
application_logic.L3: CULTURE & EXCELLENCE (8+ weeks). Integrate risk assessment into daily operations through pre-shift safety checks. Establish a health and safety committee that meets quarterly to review risks, near-misses, and improvement ideas. Conduct annual external health and safety audits. Benchmark accident rates against industry data from HSE hospitality statistics. Invest in additional controls for persistent risks: anti-fatigue mats, burn-resistant gloves, automatic wet floor signs, cable management solutions. Develop a culture where any team member can raise a safety concern without hesitation.

L1_setup.title: Immediate Actions
actions: Download or create a risk assessment template suitable for cafe environments
Conduct a full hazard identification walk-through of every area of the premises
Rate each identified hazard for likelihood and severity
Document control measures for every medium and high risk with assigned responsibility
Brief all staff on key risks and control measures in a team meeting
File the completed risk assessment and set an annual review date
Ensure the accident book (BI 510) is available and all staff know where it is

L2_build.title: System Development
actions: Develop Health and Safety SOPs for top risk categories
Introduce a near-miss reporting system
Conduct specific risk assessments for vulnerable groups
Review insurance coverage against identified risks
Schedule quarterly risk assessment reviews
Designate and train a health and safety lead
Create a COSHH register for all cleaning chemicals and hazardous substances

L3_embed.title: Culture Embedding
actions: Integrate pre-shift safety checks into daily opening routines
Establish a quarterly health and safety review meeting
Conduct annual external health and safety audits
Benchmark accident rates against HSE hospitality industry data
Invest in additional physical controls for persistent risk areas
Build a culture where near-miss reporting is encouraged and celebrated

tools.description: Comprehensive risk assessment template pre-populated with common cafe hazards organised by area (bar, kitchen, storage, customer area, exterior), with columns for hazard, who is at risk, existing controls, risk rating, additional controls needed, responsible person, and review date.

Simple one-page form for recording near-miss incidents: date, time, location, description of what happened, what prevented harm, and suggested improvement. Anonymity option included to encourage reporting.

tools.tool_name: Health and Safety SOP Library Index
tools.description: Master index of all health and safety SOPs covering burns prevention, manual handling, slip prevention, COSHH, electrical safety, and lone working, with version control and review schedule.
Register of all hazardous substances used in the cafe (cleaning chemicals, descalers, sanitisers) with safety data sheets, storage requirements, PPE requirements, and first aid measures.
Quarterly review checklist covering all risk assessment entries, near-miss trends, accident book review, control measure effectiveness, and actions arising. Signed off by owner.

failure_modes: Risk assessment is completed once and never reviewed, becoming outdated as the cafe changes equipment, layout, or activities
Risk assessment is a paper exercise that does not translate into actual workplace practices or staff awareness
Near-misses go unreported because staff fear blame, allowing hazards to persist until a serious accident occurs
Control measures are documented but not implemented or enforced, creating a false sense of security
kpis: Risk assessment completed and documented with zero overdue review dates
All staff briefed on relevant risks within one week of starting employment
Near-miss reports submitted at a rate indicating genuine engagement (minimum 1 per quarter per 5 staff)
Zero HSE enforcement notices or improvement orders
constraints: Employers with 5 or more employees must record the risk assessment in writing
Risk assessment must be reviewed at least annually or whenever there is a significant change
An accident book (BI 510) must be maintained and RIDDOR-reportable incidents reported within statutory timescales

time_load.L2: 8 hours (SOP development, COSHH register, near-miss system setup)

review_protocol: Annual full review of risk assessment document. Quarterly review of near-miss reports and accident book. Immediate review after any accident, near-miss involving injury, or significant change to premises or equipment. Staff briefing whenever risk assessment is updated.

meta.summary_for_humans: This block delivers a comprehensive workplace risk assessment covering all cafe hazards, with documented control measures, near-miss reporting, and a structured review cycle.
implementation_risks: Owner may underestimate the breadth of hazards in a cafe environment
Risk assessment can feel bureaucratic for small teams — keep it practical and cafe-specific
Without near-miss reporting, the risk assessment only captures known hazards, not emerging ones
meta.notes_for_engine: Foundation block for the regulatory governance module. Activate before or alongside B184 (fire safety) and B185 (licensing). Flag urgently if signals indicate workplace accidents, HSE visits, or missing documentation.
